title: Research
text:
My research focuses on flows through collapsible channels: problems in which a fluid interacts nonlinearly with an elastic solid. I am mainly interested in the numerical modelling of these systems, aiming to capture their dynamical behaviour, study the effect of system parameters, and characterise the physical phenomena that emerge.


Dynamical Anlysis of Collapsible Channels with Hyperelastic Walls (2026)
Supervisors: Prof. Alfonso Miguel Gañán Calvo, Prof. Miguel Ángel Herrada Gutierrez — University of Sevilla
Abstract: We present a numerical study of collapsible channels with different geometries. We analyse and characterise families of steady solutions and investigate their linear stability under variations in physical parameters. The computations are carried out using JaM, a numerical framework based on the use of analytical Jacobians and sparse collocation matrices, which allows the modelling of fluid–solid interaction problems under large deformations.
Download PDF Browse HTML version

Interactive HTML version built with Quarto — providing browsable chapters with embedded figures and code.

title: Spectral Analysis of Turbulence
Spectral Analysis of Turbulence
Spectral Analysis of Turbulence
Spectral Analysis of Turbulence
text:
Key findings
The analysis confirms:

The k^{-5/3} scaling spans approximately one decade in wavenumber space (10 \leq k \leq 100)
The Kolmogorov constant C_K \approx 1.52, consistent with literature values
The dissipation range onset occurs at k\eta \approx 0.1, where \eta is the Kolmogorov length scale

title: Spectral Analysis of Turbulence
text:
References

Pope, S. B. (2000). Turbulent Flows. Cambridge University Press.
Ishihara, T., Gotoh, T., & Kaneda, Y. (2009). Study of high-Reynolds number isotropic turbulence by direct numerical simulation. Annual Review of Fluid Mechanics, 41, 165–180.

title: Emiliano G. Herrer
text:
I am a PhD candidate (currently finishing!) in Fluid Mechanics at University of Sevilla, working on computational fluid dynamics (CFD) with applications in fluid-solid interaction problems.
More specifically, my research focus on the numerical modelling of flows through collapsible channels, using different software as Matlab, Julia and C++ to perform different kind of simulations. Check my code section to take a look to some examples of what I do!
I am passionate about open-source scientific computing, reproducible research, and making CFD tools accessible to the community.
Interests: CFD, Flud Mechanics, Elasticity, Scientific Computing
Contact: emiherrer@gmail.com

title: Lid-Driven Cavity Benchmark
text:
The lid-driven cavity is a classic benchmark problem in computational fluid dynamics. We solve the incompressible Navier-Stokes equations in a square domain \Omega = [0,1]^2 with the top wall moving at velocity u = 1 and all other walls stationary (no-slip).
The governing equations are:

\frac{\partial \mathbf{u}}{\partial t} + (\mathbf{u} \cdot \nabla)\mathbf{u} = -\nabla p + \frac{1}{Re}\nabla^2\mathbf{u}


\nabla \cdot \mathbf{u} = 0

title: Lid-Driven Cavity Benchmark
text:
The lid-driven cavity is a classic benchmark problem in computational fluid dynamics. We solve the incompressible Navier-Stokes equations in a square domain \Omega = [0,1]^2 with the top wall moving at velocity u = 1 and all other walls stationary (no-slip).
The governing equations are:

\frac{\partial \mathbf{u}}{\partial t} + (\mathbf{u} \cdot \nabla)\mathbf{u} = -\nabla p + \frac{1}{Re}\nabla^2\mathbf{u}


\nabla \cdot \mathbf{u} = 0

title: Lid-Driven Cavity Benchmark
text:
Setup

# If you use your own solver, import it here
# using MyNSSolver

# For this example, we'll use standard Julia packages
using LinearAlgebra
using Printf

# Simulation parameters
Re_values = [100, 400, 1000]
N = 128  # Grid points per direction

128

title: Lid-Driven Cavity Benchmark
text:
Results

Velocity profiles at Re = 100
The vertical velocity component v along the horizontal centreline, and the horizontal velocity component u along the vertical centreline, are compared against the reference data from Ghia et al. (1982).

Code
# ── Example: replace this with your actual simulation results ──
# Here we generate placeholder data to show the structure

# Reference data from Ghia et al. (1982) - u along vertical centreline at Re=100
ghia_y = [0.0, 0.0547, 0.0625, 0.0703, 0.1016, 0.1719, 0.2813, 
          0.4531, 0.5, 0.6172, 0.7344, 0.8516, 0.9531, 0.9609, 
          0.9688, 0.9766, 1.0]
ghia_u = [0.0, -0.03717, -0.04192, -0.04775, -0.06434, -0.10150, 
          -0.15662, -0.21090, -0.20581, -0.13641, 0.00332, 0.23151, 
          0.68717, 0.73722, 0.78871, 0.84123, 1.0]

# Your simulation results would go here:
# sim_y, sim_u = solve_cavity(Re=100, N=128)

# For demonstration, we just print the reference data
println("Ghia et al. (1982) reference data loaded successfully")
println("Number of reference points: $(length(ghia_y))")

# ── When you have plotting packages: ──
# using CairoMakie
# fig = Figure(size=(500, 400))
# ax = Axis(fig[1,1], xlabel="u", ylabel="y", title="Re = 100")
# scatter!(ax, ghia_u, ghia_y, label="Ghia et al.", marker=:circle)
# lines!(ax, sim_u, sim_y, label="Present (N=$N)", color=:red)
# axislegend(ax)
# fig





Ghia et al. (1982) reference data loaded successfully
Number of reference points: 17



Figure 1





Convergence analysis

Code
# Example mesh convergence data
grid_sizes = [16, 32, 64, 128, 256]
errors = [0.0512, 0.0134, 0.00341, 0.000862, 0.000217]

# Compute convergence order
for i in 2:length(errors)
    order = log(errors[i-1]/errors[i]) / log(grid_sizes[i]/grid_sizes[i-1])
    @printf("N = %3d → %3d : order = %.2f\n", grid_sizes[i-1], grid_sizes[i], order)
end





N =  16 →  32 : order = 1.93
N =  32 →  64 : order = 1.97
N =  64 → 128 : order = 1.98
N = 128 → 256 : order = 1.99



Figure 2

title: Lid-Driven Cavity Benchmark
text:

Ghia, U., Ghia, K. N., & Shin, C. T. (1982). High-Re solutions for incompressible flow using the Navier-Stokes equations and a multigrid method. Journal of Computational Physics, 48(3), 387–411.

title: Code
text:
Solvers & Libraries

ProjectName 🌊
Description of your main code project. For example: A high-order finite volume solver for incompressible Navier-Stokes equations, implemented in Julia. Features adaptive mesh refinement, parallel computation via MPI, and post-processing tools.
Language: Julia | License: MIT
[Documentation] [GitHub]


AnotherProject 🔧
MATLAB toolbox for [specific task]. For example: A collection of MATLAB functions for spectral analysis of turbulent flow data, including energy spectra computation, structure functions, and proper orthogonal decomposition (POD).
Language: MATLAB | License: GPL-3.0
[GitHub]


UtilityScripts ⚙️
Miscellaneous utilities. Post-processing scripts, mesh generation tools, and visualization helpers used across my research projects.
Languages: Julia, MATLAB, Python
[GitHub]
